content: The sky appears blue due to Rayleigh scattering. Sunlight contains all visible colors. When it enters Earth's atmosphere, it collides with gas molecules like nitrogen and oxygen. Blue light has shorter wavelengths and scatters more strongly than longer wavelengths like red or yellow. This scattered blue light reaches our eyes from every direction, creating the blue appearance. During sunrise or sunset, sunlight travels through more atmosphere, scattering blue light away before it reaches us, allowing reds and oranges to dominate.

Sunlight appears white but contains all colors of the visible spectrum. As this light passes through Earth's atmosphere, it collides with gas molecules like nitrogen and oxygen. This interaction causes the light to scatter in different directions, a phenomenon known as Rayleigh scattering.

Shorter wavelengths, such as blue and violet, scatter much more strongly than longer wavelengths like red or yellow. Although violet light scatters even more than blue, human eyes are more sensitive to blue light, and the sun emits more blue light than violet. Consequently, when we look up, the scattered blue light reaches our eyes from all directions, making the sky appear blue.

The sky appears blue due to Rayleigh scattering. Sunlight contains all colors of the rainbow, but blue light travels in shorter, smaller waves. When sunlight reaches Earth's atmosphere, these shorter waves scatter more strongly than longer wavelengths like red or yellow when they collide with gas molecules.

Although violet light scatters even more, human eyes are more sensitive to blue, and the sun emits more blue light than violet. Consequently, the scattered blue light reaches our eyes from every direction in the sky. This scattering effect is most intense when the sun is high overhead, creating the characteristic blue dome we observe during the day.
